name: Flipkart Algorithm Selection Tool
scenario: Flipkart's engineering team is selecting sorting and search algorithms for Big Billion Days. Last year, a naive quicksort caused a 47-minute outage on sorted product catalog data (worst case O(n²)). Build an Algorithm Selection Tool that analyzes inputs and recommends the safest, fastest algorithm for each scenario.

core.description: AlgorithmProfile record: name, bestCase, worstCase, averageCase, space, stable. At minimum: quicksort, mergesort, insertionsort, binarysearch, hashmap. All fields accurate.

InputAnalyzer.analyze(int[] arr) → returns InputType enum: SORTED, REVERSE_SORTED, RANDOM, NEARLY_SORTED. Detection: sorted if arr[i]<=arr[i+1] always; reverse if arr[i]>=arr[i+1]; nearly sorted if <5% elements out of place.

AlgorithmSelector.recommend(InputType, int n) → returns best algorithm for that input + reason. E.g., SORTED + large n → timsort/insertionsort (O(n) best case). RANDOM + large n → quicksort with random pivot.
AmortizedDemo: show ArrayList.add() amortized O(1) by tracking total cost across 1000 adds. Verify total_cost/n < 3.
@SpringBootTest (4 tests): SORTED detected correctly, quicksort worst triggered on sorted, recommendation avoids quicksort for sorted, amortized ratio < 3. All pass.
README: explains the Flipkart quicksort incident — what went wrong, why, and how the tool prevents it.

bonus.description: QuicksortComparison: naive vs random-pivot on sorted n=2000. Count comparisons for each. Naive should be ≥ 10x more comparisons than random-pivot. Verify both sort correctly.
Union-Find with path compression: 1M operations on n=100K elements in under 2 seconds. Print operations/second.
InsertionSortBestCase: show sorted input → O(n) comparisons (≈ n). Random input → O(n²) comparisons (≈ n²/4). Ratio should be ≥ n/8.

ai_policy_explanation: 5 hints. Worst case vs average case: quicksort's O(n²) worst case requires a SPECIFIC input pattern — sorted array with last-element pivot. Random input → average O(n log n). Your InputAnalyzer must detect this trigger. Amortized O(1): track cumulative cost, not per-operation cost. Total cost for n ArrayList.add() calls = 2n (geometric series of resize costs). Divide by n → 2 = O(1) amortized. Insertion sort O(n) best case: on already-sorted input, the inner while loop never executes (arr[j] <= key always). Count inner-loop iterations: sorted → 0 per element → O(n) total. Nearly sorted detection: count inversions or elements where arr[i] > arr[i+1]. If < 5% of n, classify as NEARLY_SORTED.
